desc: Doha is a polished Gulf capital where museums, waterfront promenades, souqs, and glossy hotel districts all sit inside a compact urban footprint. It works best as a winter-sun city break or long stopover rather than a summer classic, because the cultural core is strong but the heat from late spring into early autumn can dominate everything except indoor plans.
hls: Souq Waqif and the adjoining old-core atmosphere
Museum of Islamic Art on the Corniche
Katara Cultural Village between West Bay and The Pearl
Best seasonal window from November to March

todo.name: Souq Waqif evening wander
todo.desc: Save the souq for late afternoon into night, when the lanes, cafes, and market stalls feel most alive.

todo.name: Museum of Islamic Art
todo.desc: Use the I.M. Pei landmark for both the collection and the skyline-and-Corniche setting around it.

todo.name: Corniche walk
todo.desc: Walk the waterfront in the cooler part of the day for skyline views and a sense of Doha's layout.

todo.name: Katara Cultural Village
todo.desc: Pair galleries, public art, beach edges, and cafe stops in one of Doha's most visitor-friendly districts.

todo.name: National Museum of Qatar
todo.desc: Add the dramatic desert-rose building for a broader read on Qatari identity and state-building.

todo.name: One polished rooftop or hotel-bar night
todo.desc: Doha's licensed hotel scene is the easiest way to sample the city's upscale after-dark side.

wifi.note: Very strong in hotels, malls, cafes, airport lounges, and business districts; one of the easier Middle East city bases for reliable remote-work connectivity.

prac.fltNote: Doha itself has current nonstop service from London Gatwick into Hamad International, so the destination-level LGW direct flag is genuinely true, while LCY remains false. The airport is close enough to make short stopovers practical, but the real demand spike comes in the November-March comfort season and during major events, not in the punishing midsummer heat.
prac.lang: Arabic is the official language; English is widely used across hotels, restaurants, museums, and transport.
prac.tz: AST (UTC+3) year-round; no daylight saving time.
prac.visa: Qatar uses nationality-based entry rules, with many visitors able to enter visa-free or via simple pre-arrival processes, but passport-specific requirements should still be checked before travel.
alerts: From roughly May to September, the heat can make daytime walking feel brutal even for experienced hot-weather travellers.
During Ramadan, opening hours, nightlife rhythm, and dining patterns shift noticeably, so daytime expectations need adjusting.
Alcohol is limited to licensed venues such as hotels, bars, and certain restaurants rather than everyday street-level options.
Souq Waqif and top museums are much more enjoyable in evening or early-day slots than in midday sun.

sweet: November to March is Doha's clear sweet spot, with comfortable temperatures for the Corniche, souqs, and museum-hopping; April and late October can still work, but summer is a specialist choice only.
